step_1_context.text: Walking through the park, she noticed a strange bird. Exhausted by the journey, they fell asleep immediately.

step_2_explanation.simple_rule: Participle clauses shorten sentences by replacing a relative clause or time clause. Present participle (-ing) for active meaning, past participle (-ed) for passive.
step_2_explanation.formula: Active: Verb-ing + ..., subject + verb. Passive: Past participle + ..., subject + verb.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: The subject of the participle must match the subject of the main clause. 'Walking home, the rain started' is wrong because rain was not walking.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: ___ the book, she returned it to the library.
options: Both A and B are correct
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: 'Having finished' (perfect participle) shows the action was completed before the main action.

step_3_interactive_exercises.incorrect_sentence: Driving to work, the accident happened.
step_3_interactive_exercises.corrected_sentence: Driving to work, he saw an accident happen.
step_3_interactive_exercises.error_analysis: The accident was not driving. The subject of the participle must match the main clause subject.
